exemplar.title: An Experiment to Investigate the Effect of Interference of Rehearsal on Memory Recall

detailedExemplarData.title: An Experiment to Investigate the Effect of Interference of Rehearsal on Memory Recall

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear statement of aim with well-explained theoretical and real-world relevance
Detailed description of the multi-store model linked explicitly to the present investigation
Hypotheses operationalize retention intervals and recall accuracy effectively
weaknesses: Minor factual error conflating sensory and short-term memory durations
Explanation of retroactive interference is incomplete
Secondary independent variable (presence/absence of interference) could be more clearly defined
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction meets most of the top-band descriptorsâaim and relevance are explicitly explained, the theory/model is well described and connected, and variables are operationalized. However, factual lapses about sensory memory and truncated explanations prevent a full bandâ5â6 award, resulting in a score of 4.

strengths: Independent-measures design is not only described but justified to avoid order effects
Opportunity sampling and participant characteristics are identified with brief rationale
Key controls and materials are listed with some purposes explained
weaknesses: Insufficient detail on random allocation procedure
Limited monitoring description for procedural compliance
Discussion of how sampling affects external validity is too brief
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While all three strands reach the mid-band threshold by describing and justifying design, sampling, and controls, the explanations lack depthâespecially around allocation, compliance monitoring and validity implicationsâwarranting a score of 2 out of 4.

strengths: Appropriate use of ANOVA alongside means and standard deviations
Graphical presentation directly addresses the hypothesis with error bars
Findings are linked to hypothesis and null-hypothesis testing
weaknesses: Minor errors in ANOVA table and p-value wording
Graph title and axis labels are incomplete
Interpretation contains a contradiction between reported results and hypothesis
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Statistical methods and graphical techniques are appropriate and broadly accurate, and interpretations link to the hypothesis. However, inconsistencies in reporting, presentation flaws and contradictory statements mean the work sits solidly in the mid-band, earning a 3.

strengths: Findings are discussed with reference to Peterson & Petersonâs model
Strengths and limitations are stated, explained and directly relevant
Modifications are explicitly linked to limitations with clear justification
weaknesses: Discussion lacks deeper critical evaluation of alternative explanations
Some recommendations (e.g. power analysis) are suggested but not fully developed
Further exploration of validity impacts could strengthen the evaluation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation integrates theory, explains strengths/limitations in context, and ties modifications to specific issuesâfulfilling most top-band criteria. A more critical depth in the discussion of alternative explanations and expanded methodological detail is needed to reach the maximum, resulting in a 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: A clustered bar chart with error bars compares recall across intervals for both groups, directly addressing the hypothesis.  Labels and units are present, yet the title is fragmented and axis formatting is basic.  Small presentation flaws situate the work in the 3â4 band, lower end.

The student interprets differences in means and SDs, discusses the graphical trend, reports the ANOVA outcome, and explicitly links these interpretations to the rejection of the null and confirmation of the hypothesis.  Because both data discussion and linkage are present and mostly accurate, the strand achieves the top band, though not flawless enough for 6.

Both hypotheses are presented.  The IV (retention interval) and DV (accuracy of recalled trigrams) are explicitly identified and operationalised through specific timings (3â18 s) and a scored recall measure.  This fulfils the highest-band criteria; slight ambiguity over the secondary IV (presence/absence of interference) prevents awarding a 6.

Opportunity sampling of two Grade-10 classes is stated and the rationale (accessibility, diversity, parental consent) is explained.  The effect on generalisability is acknowledged, meeting the âexplainedâ criterion of the upper band, though no alternative techniques are evaluated, so a middle score of 3 is appropriate.

The multi-store model is described in detail (sensory, STM, LTM, capacities and durations) and Peterson & Peterson (1959) is summarised to illustrate how rehearsal prevents decay.  The student then explicitly links that theoretical framework to the present replication and to the IV (retention interval/interference).  This meets the requirement of describing the theory and explaining its connection to the investigation, meriting the 5â6 band; some minor factual slips ("sensoryâ¦ up to 18 s") keep the score at 5.

Findings are related back to Peterson & Peterson and the multi-store model, demonstrating theoretical integration.  The discussion, however, is concise and lacks critical depth about alternative explanations, positioning it solidly in the 3â4 band.

The student clearly states the aim: to replicate Peterson & Peterson (1959) and examine the effect of interference on STM recall.  In the opening two paragraphs the practical and theoretical relevance of understanding interference (educational practice, eyewitness testimony, support for the multi-store model) is explicitly explained, not merely mentioned.  This alignment between aim and broader significance satisfies the descriptor for the top band, though minor wording lapses prevent awarding the maximum 6.

Several strengths (high control, anonymity, randomised trigrams) and limitations (proximity of participants, unequal group sizes, homogenous sample) are identified and each is explained in terms of its impact on validity or generalisability.  This moves beyond listing into clear explanation, matching the 5â6 band, mid-level.

Key controls (standardised script, quiet room, five-second recall window) and materials (timer, consent form, handouts) are listed and their purposes briefly justified, satisfying the requirement for explanation.  However, description of how these controls were monitored is brief; thus a solid but not top performance within the 3â4 band.

Proposed modifications (increase sample size, spacing participants, single experimenter for pronunciation) are each explicitly tied to the corresponding limitation and justified regarding how they would improve data quality.  This explicit linkage and rationale fulfil the descriptor for the top band, albeit without exhaustive methodological detail, warranting a 5.

The independent-measures design is not only described but justified (avoiding order effects and fatigue).  While this constitutes an explanation, detail on exactly how random allocation occurred is thin, so the strand comfortably fits the 3â4 band but does not reach exemplary depth for full 4 marks.

Means, standard deviations and a two-way ANOVA are provided.  The test is appropriate for two IVs (retention interval and interference) and the p-value interpretation leads to correct rejection of the null, though the wording reverses critical vs. p value and the raw ANOVA table shows minor inconsistencies.  These small errors place the work in the 3â4 band, top end.

annotations.comment: The introduction effectively demonstrates real-world relevance by connecting memory research to educational strategies and eyewitness testimony, strengthening the investigationâs context.
The claim that sensory memory records information for up to 18 seconds confuses sensory memory with shortâterm memory; sensory memory's duration is typically under 1 second.
The summary of proactive and retroactive interference in the second paragraph is truncated; please complete the explanation of retroactive interference to clarify how new information impedes recall.
The hypothesis correctly operationalizes retention intervals, but could you clarify how the presence/absence of interference functions as a secondary independent variable?
The independent measures design is justified for minimizing order effects, yet more detail on the procedure for random allocation to conditions would strengthen the research design explanation.

Describing only 'absence or presence of interference' oversimplifies the independent variable; consider explicitly defining both retention interval and interference conditions in variable operationalization.
Opportunity sampling and claims of diversity are noted, but the sampleâs narrow age homogeneity may limit generalizability; discuss how age uniformity affects external validity.
While key variables (script, quiet room, fiveâsecond recall) are described, there is insufficient detail on how you monitored participant compliance to ensure these controls were maintained.
The statement 'There were five trials for each time interval' ends abruptly; specify whether these trials include practice runs and clarify total trial count in the procedure.
Including the instruction for participants to count aloud as evidence of compliance monitoring demonstrates thoughtful consideration of procedural controls.
The rationale that using pens prevents altering answers is asserted but not explained; please elaborate how pens reduce potential bias or data manipulation.
Provide more detail on the transition from practice to real trials, such as criteria for participant readiness, to ensure procedural clarity.
You assert 'low skewness indicates that the mean is credible,' but skewness statistics are not reported; please include skewness values or justify this claim.
The term 'significant 13âpoint difference' could be misinterpreted; distinguish clearly between practical and statistical significance and reference an appropriate test.
The explanation of standard deviation as a dispersion measure effectively justifies your choice of descriptive statistics and demonstrates understanding of data variability.
The clustered bar chart with error bars directly addresses the hypothesis by comparing conditions across retention intervals in a clear visual format.
Improve the graph title to fully label both groups and include the independent variable, for example 'ShortâTerm Memory Recall by Group across Retention Intervals'.
The interpretation states that time had no significant impact on retention, which contradicts your hypothesis and ANOVA results; reconcile this inconsistency in your discussion.
The evaluation discussion effectively links findings back to the multiâstore model, reinforcing the theoretical underpinnings of the replication.
Identifying labâbased internal validity as a strength, with consistent timing and anonymity measures, shows a solid understanding of experimental rigor.
When discussing sample limitations, elaborate on how the small and uneven group sizes might specifically influence both internal and external validity.
Linking the proposed pronunciation adjustment ('zed') directly to letterâdistinction issues demonstrates a wellâjustified modification related to your procedural limitation.
Consider conducting an a priori power analysis to justify the proposed increase from 24 to 30 participants and strengthen your modification rationale.
For the limitation of participants sitting too close, propose a specific spatial arrangement or seating protocol to prevent copying, making the modification explicit.
Beyond restating conclusions, consider extending your investigation by comparing proactive versus retroactive interference to deepen your analysis in future studies.
